text: The surgeon positions the incision site approximately two inches supraumbilical, ensuring it is not excessively high.
The surgeon cleans the laparoscopic scope before inserting it into the abdominal cavity for a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the laparoscopic view inside the abdominal cavity, preparing to initiate insufflation by starting the gas flow.
The surgeon examines the appendicular area, observing visible adhesions around the appendix.
The surgeon confirms the fixed placement of the surgical ports, which are positioned for optimal access during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgical setup includes two ports positioned on the surgeon's side and one port on the assistant's side, facilitating access to the operative field.
The lower port is positioned one inch above and medial to the anterior superior iliac spine, providing access for the surgical instruments.
The surgeon observes colitic adhesions, which are visible as fibrous bands connecting the colon to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon visually identifies the fallopian tube on one side, using it as a landmark for the procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the left-sided fallopian tube, noting its position relative to the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a fibroid located on the posterior wall of the uterus.
The surgeon visually identifies the right fallopian tube, noting its position relative to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies a large fibroid located on the anterior wall and another large fibroid on the posterior wall of the uterus.
The surgeon points out a corneal fibroid on the uterus, highlighting its location and appearance.
The surgeon visually identifies the fallopian tube extending over the uterine surface, likely in proximity to the previously mentioned fibroids.
The surgeon identifies one small fibroid and two large fibroids on the uterine wall, highlighting their size and location.
The surgeon identifies one small fibroid and one seedling fibroid on the uterus, in addition to previously noted larger fibroids on the anterior and posterior walls.
The surgeon visually identifies three small fibroids and two large fibroids within the uterine tissue.
The vasopressor injection needle is introduced into the surgical field, likely targeting the fibroid tissue for vasoconstriction.
The surgeon prepares to inject a vasopressor into the myometrium, targeting the area around the fibroids.
The surgeon injects a vasopressor half an inch into the anterior myometrium using a needle, targeting the specified area for treatment.
The vasopressor injection needle is inserted half an inch into the myometrium, followed by aspiration to ensure proper placement.
The surgeon injects 10 ml of vasopressor into the myometrium using a needle, with the fluid visibly entering the tissue.
The surgeon injects thirty milliliters of fluid into the anterior myometrium, visibly expanding the tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon injects approximately fifty milliliters of solution into the uterine tissue, monitoring the volume administered during the myomectomy procedure.
The upper fibroid appears white, indicating reduced blood flow or ischemia in the tissue.
The surgeon injects a solution into the anterior wall of the uterus, reaching the posterior myometrium.
The surgeon prepares to remove the posterior fibroid, noting the visible effects of vasopressin and aqua dissection on the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon begins suturing the incisions made during the myomectomy procedure, ensuring each suture is placed precisely to close the surgical site.
The video shows the uterus with a distorted contour due to the presence of multiple fibroids.
The surgeon prepares to separate and remove the fibroid from the distorted uterine contour, focusing on the visible fibroid tissue.
The surgeon makes an elliptical incision on the uterine wall to facilitate effective suturing.
The surgeon observes a degenerated fibroid with visible myometrial hypertrophy, a common characteristic in fibroids.
The surgeon observes persistent bleeding and prepares to remove a fibroid from the uterine wall before beginning the suturing process.
The surgeon identifies a larger fibroid located on the anterior side of the uterus.
The fibroid is visible as the saline solution surrounds it, highlighting its contours against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon avoids trimming the fibroid, noting that the broad ligament tissue available for suturing decreases once the fibroid is delivered out.
The surgeon performs a minor trimming of the tissue surrounding the fibroid, preparing the area for further surgical steps.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw to manipulate the fibroid, which is visible due to the surrounding saline solution.
The surgeon uses a tooth needle holder to provide counter traction on the fibroid, ensuring even distribution of force during manipulation.
The surgeon advances the myoma screw on the fibroid, facilitating its removal. After the fibroid is extracted, the uterus appears normal.
The myoma screw is visibly bent during the procedure.
The surgeon applies force to the myoma screw, which visibly bends due to the excessive pressure required during the procedure.

The surgeon observes the surgical field, noting no bleeding anteriorly but significant bleeding posteriorly, prompting cauterization. The surgeon advises against tunneling incisions due to potential myometrial trauma.
The surgeon makes a small incision on the posterior uterine wall to remove the fibroid, using a technique that minimizes tissue trauma.

Approximately 50 mL of blood loss is observed from the posterior fibroid. The surgeon uses a double needle barb suture, specifically a 24 by 4 centimeter Stratafix suture from Johnson & Johnson, to address the bleeding.
The surgeon manipulates a needle, with one positioned outside the abdominal wall, while attempting to pull more of the suture through the tissue.
The surgeon begins suturing the posterior wall, followed by the anterior wall, and plans to locate the smaller fibroid on the anterior wall for removal.
The surgeon makes a full-thickness incision on the uterine wall, ensuring it is not excessively deep, to access the fibroid.
The surgeon begins suturing the incision site in a double layer technique, ensuring secure closure of the tissue layers.
The surgeon assesses the depth of the incision, determining that a double layer closure will be adequate for the current surgical site.
The surgeon retracts the tissue by holding the suture, using the weight of the needle holder to elevate the tissue for better access.
The surgeon takes a full-thickness bite with the suture needle, ensuring secure closure of the tissue layers.
The surgeon positions the tissue upside down and completes the suturing of the deeper layer, preparing for the next step in closure.
The surgeon performs a baseball stitch, also known as an inside-out stitch, to close the upper part of the incision, ensuring secure tissue approximation.
The surgeon turns the needle and holds it in position, preparing for the next stitch in the closure process.
The surgeon turns the needle and performs an inside-out stitch technique, manipulating the needle to pass through the tissue layers.

The surgeon uses a longer thread to pass the needle gently through the tissue, ensuring it emerges at the precise location desired for the suture line. One part of the suture is completed, and the needle is carefully extracted from the tissue.
The surgeon completes the closure of the upper part of the incision using a baseball stitch technique, returning to the initial incision line.
The surgeon takes two additional sutures to complete the final closure of the incision site.
The surgeon observes that the tissue is cut through marginally, indicating a precise incision or separation at the surgical site.
The surgeon gently tightens the suture, achieving sufficient closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut through the tissue, following previous adjustments to the tension applied.
The surgeon completes the posterior valve suturing and proceeds to cut the excess thread using surgical scissors.
The surgeon pulls the second needle into the surgical field, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the effect of vasopressin on the uterine tissue, which appears blanched and less vascularized, indicating reduced blood flow in the surgical field.
The anterior part of the surgical field shows no signs of bleeding, indicating effective hemostasis.
The surgeon observes that the incision has shifted to a vertical orientation, which may complicate the closure process.
The fibroid is removed, and the surrounding tissue returns to its normal position, revealing a vertical incision.
The surgeon completes the first layer of closure after removing adenomyotic tissue, noting the toughness of the tissue and the challenge of suturing due to the lack of a clear plane.
The surgeon performs a baseball stitch to close the incision site, ensuring the edges of the tissue are neatly approximated.
The surgeon performs an inside-out suture technique, threading the suture material through the tissue layers from the interior to the exterior, progressing downward along the surgical site.
The surgeon takes two more suturing bites on the uterine tissue, preparing to tie the knot with a short thread, which may pose a challenge due to its length.
The surgeon completes suturing both the anterior and posterior surfaces, ensuring closure of the surgical site.
The surgical field shows the removal of three fibroids, with visible areas where the fibroids were excised.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw and Grasperani wash to manipulate and clean the surgical field, preparing for further fibroid removal.
The surgeon examines a peritoneal fold where blood has visibly collected, assessing the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon injects the remaining solution into the myometrium, observing the fluid leaking back from the cut open myometrium.
The uterus is observed turning white, indicating a change in tissue coloration during the procedure.
The surgeon makes a transverse incision on the uterus, preparing to place figure-of-eight sutures for closure.
A small seedling fibroid is visible, possibly revealed by aqua dissection.
The surgeon uses a figure-of-eight suture technique to close the incision on the anterior wall, ensuring adequate closure with a single suture.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to coagulate tissue without suturing, indicating that two figure-of-eight sutures or a continuous suture will be sufficient for closure.
The surgeon takes an additional suture bite using a cutting needle, ensuring precise placement and control in the tissue.
The surgeon gently tightens the suture step by step to ensure it remains secure without cutting through the tissue.
The surgeon gently tightens the suture line, causing it to branch and become visibly white, indicating sufficient tension.
The surgeon applies a bipolar instrument to the tissue, which visibly causes damage to the surrounding area.
The surgeon performs surface coagulation on a seedling fibroid, applying energy to the surface tissue.
The surgical field is quickly irrigated, followed by the morcellation of the tissue.
Mild oozing is observed at the surgical site, with blood seeping from the tissue compared to the usual output.
The fimbria ovarica is visible without any accessory fat present.
The surgeon removes accumulated blood from the pouch of Douglas, clearing the surgical field.
The surgeon reassesses the amount of blood present in the pouch following the completion of morselation, visually inspecting the surgical field for any residual bleeding.
The surgeon picks up two small tissue pieces from the surgical field.
A long strip of tissue is being extracted from the surgical site during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon removes the morcellator from the surgical field after completing the morcellation process.
The surgeon places their thumb on the laparoscopic port to prevent leakage during the procedure.
The surgeon re-inserts the 5mm port into the surgical site.
The surgeon examines the amount of blood collected in the pouch, visually assessing the extent of bleeding post-morcellation.
The surgeon observes minimal oozing from the puncture site, ensuring not to over-coagulate or touch the suture material with the cautery device.
The surgeon observes slight oozing from the needle puncture site at the end of suturing. A gentle touch with the instrument stops the bleeding entirely at the needle point.
The surgeon observes a very mild ooze at the puncture site and decides to leave it without further intervention.
The surgeon injects 10 cc of ropivacaine into the port site and another 10 cc into the peritoneal cavity, administering the anesthetic just above the peritoneum and below the muscle layer.
The needle is inserted directly into the surgical site.
The surgeon injects ropivacaine just above the peritoneum, allowing the anesthetic to spread in the targeted area beneath the muscle layer.
The surgeon prepares to close the morcellator port, ensuring proper closure of the incision site following the procedure.
The surgeon places the morcellator into the surgical field, preparing for tissue removal.
The surgeon carefully inserts the needle, avoiding the muscle tissue, and takes a bite of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully places the needle inside the abdominal cavity with insufflation on, ensuring proper positioning for the procedure.
The surgeon carefully knots the muscle tissue, ensuring precision with each stitch.
